description: case6 + realistic padding to ~30000 tokens. Padding fragments from prior_iteration / abandoned_tangent / vendor_comparison / repetition / exploratory pools; NOT in GT.

chat.content: We're spec'ing a graph-processing system for a 50-billion-edge knowledge graph. Queries come in two flavors: ad-hoc analytical queries (latency tolerant, complex), and online lookups (latency-critical, simple). We need to figure out the architecture before the next quarterly planning. The product team wants clarity on what's possible by end of week.
Two-flavor traffic suggests two separate stores or a single store with smart routing. Do we have a target latency for each? And what's the read-write ratio for each flavor?
Random thought: what if we used homomorphic encryption so the cloud provider can't see our graph data even if they wanted to?
Homomorphic encryption for graph operations is hundreds of times slower than plaintext. Latency budget would be destroyed.
Yeah, the latency tradeoff makes it a non-starter.
HE shelved.

Online lookups: p99 under 50ms, p50 under 10ms. Analytical: under 30 seconds is fine, p99 can be a few minutes for complex multi-hop traversals. Throughput: ~5k online QPS sustained with bursts to 20k, analytical is bursty maybe 50 concurrent. Read-write: online is 95/5 reads, analytical is read-only.
Do we have any sense of what happens if our partner Alpha onboards 10x the queries we expected?
Their rate limit at the gateway would kick in. We'd see lots of 429s on their end and not be impacted ourselves.
Right, technical answer is fine. Contract is a separate conversation.
Tech side handled by rate limits.

OK. Given the 30-second tolerance on analytical, you can spill to disk on those. The 50ms p99 on online lookups effectively requires in-memory hot paths or aggressive caching. The 20k burst is the real number to design against. Have we considered separating the workloads physically?
Just want to double-check the cut-over plan — replay-based migration, not dual-write?
Replay-based from a known checkpoint. Dual-write was rejected because it would double write load.
Right.
Replay it is.

Yes, I've been leaning that way. Online uses a property-graph store with hot data in memory; analytical uses a columnar engine on the same underlying data, replicated. The question is whether the replication path is cheap enough to make this worthwhile vs a single hybrid store.
And no Friday cut-overs, no pre-holiday, no PTO?
Right, all three scheduling constraints. They're a hard rule.
Got it.
Scheduling rule confirmed.

That works but raises the consistency question: how stale can the analytical replica be? And the replication path is cheap if writes go through a clean log; it gets expensive if you have to replay graph traversals.
Earlier we discussed CRDT-based conflict resolution for cross-region writes. Did we ever resolve that?
It got absorbed into the 'no multi-region active-active for v1' decision. If we ever go active-active, CRDT design becomes a real conversation.
OK so CRDT is post-v1 territory.
Tied to active-active decision. Post-v1.
We can tolerate 5 minutes of staleness on analytical. That's a hard requirement from the data team. Anything more and they'll mistrust the results, and the trust we lose there is hard to win back.
What about NebulaGraph? It's open source from China, allegedly scales well.
NebulaGraph has good benchmarks but the documentation outside Chinese is patchy. Operational support for an English-speaking team would be a challenge.
Documentation matters. JanusGraph stays.
Nebula not pursued.
5-minute staleness is comfortable for batch replication. Online cannot be stale at all — it must be the source of truth?
What if we built a query plan analyzer that warns developers about expensive queries before they ship?
Static query analysis on Gremlin/Cypher is hard because the cost depends on data shapes. Best-effort tools exist but they're imperfect.
Sounds like nice-to-have but not essential.
Nice-to-have, post-v1.
Online is source of truth. Analytical reads from a snapshot replicated every 5 minutes. No analytical writes ever — that's a hard rule, not just for v1.
GDPR erasure still propagates within one snapshot window?
Yes, 5 minutes worst-case for analytical. Online is immediate.
5 minutes for analytical, immediate for online.
Confirmed.

Good. So decision: online store is source of truth (writes go there); analytical is a read-only replica refreshed every 5 minutes, no writes ever. What about the engine choices? You mentioned property graph for online.
I had an earlier idea about using ScyllaDB instead of Cassandra. Same wire protocol, better per-node throughput. Why didn't we pick it?
Operational maturity. Cassandra is well-understood; Scylla is a smaller ecosystem and we'd be the first in the org to run it. The throughput edge wasn't worth the unknown.
Right. We default to the more boring choice when capabilities are similar.
Cassandra it is. Scylla shelved indefinitely.
I've been considering Neo4j for the online side and DuckDB for analytical. Neo4j has good operational story; DuckDB is fast on columnar workloads and has zero ops overhead.
What about AllegroGraph? Mature semantic-web graph DB.
AllegroGraph is RDF/SPARQL-focused like Stardog. Different problem domain. We're property-graph, not semantic-web.
Right, wrong tool.
AllegroGraph not relevant.
Neo4j at 50 billion edges is well above typical deployments. Have we benchmarked it at this scale? Their published reference architectures top out far below that.
Out of curiosity: what would the system look like if we wrote it in Rust instead of using the JVM-based JanusGraph?
Rust would mean writing our own graph engine. The engineering cost is enormous — we'd be a graph-database company instead of a customer-facing analytics company. Not viable.
Yeah, that was just curiosity. Not a real proposal.
Right. JanusGraph stays.

Not at 50B. Their public material caps at single-digit billions in real customer references, and even those involved Neo4j's professional services. That's a real risk for us — we don't have the budget for Neo4j Enterprise plus their consulting.
How does our setup compare to Stardog, Ontotext, or other commercial graph platforms? Anything we're missing?

Stardog and Ontotext target the semantic-web / RDF / SPARQL world. Strong on inference and reasoning, weaker on the raw-edge-count throughput we need. Our property-graph workload doesn't benefit from their semantic strengths.
So they're solving a different problem. Not applicable.
Different problem domain. No action.

Risk worth flagging. Alternatives: TigerGraph, JanusGraph, or a custom KV-backed graph on RocksDB. JanusGraph is open-source with Cassandra/HBase backends that scale, but the query engine has been called sluggish on complex traversals. TigerGraph has good benchmarks but expensive.
And we're not adding Gremlin to the analytical side?
Correct. SQL only on analytical. Gremlin not pursued.
Right.
SQL-only on analytical.

Custom on RocksDB gives us control but engineering cost is massive — we'd be building a query planner from scratch. TigerGraph licensing is a non-starter. JanusGraph on Cassandra is the most credible scale story for our budget. Let's go with JanusGraph for online. Drop Neo4j as the online choice.
And online lookups have to be under 50ms at p99?
Yes, p99 under 50ms, p50 under 10ms, sustained at 5k QPS with bursts to 20k.
Right.
That's the latency budget.
Noted. JanusGraph on Cassandra for online. DuckDB still the analytical pick? Or has the picture shifted?
Going back to authentication: we considered using API keys instead of OAuth for service-to-service. Why did we pick mTLS in the end?

API keys are bearer tokens — anyone with the key has access. mTLS ties identity to a certificate that's harder to exfiltrate and easier to rotate. For service-to-service inside our infrastructure, mTLS is the higher bar.
Yeah, mTLS for service-to-service is correct. API keys would be a regression.
mTLS for the ingestion service stays. API keys are not on the table.

Yes, DuckDB for analytical. Snapshots from Cassandra to Parquet, DuckDB reads Parquet. DuckDB's vectorized execution handles our column-oriented analytical patterns well and runs on a single beefy node, which keeps ops simple.
Are we still encrypting both at rest and in transit?
Yes, both. Cassandra and Parquet at rest, all client connections in transit. KMS for keys.
OK.
Encryption confirmed.

Constraint to write down: snapshots must be transactionally consistent — we can't snapshot Cassandra mid-write and get torn rows in the Parquet files. That would break the data team's trust on the first incident.
Random idea: integrate with our user-data warehouse via change-data-capture so the warehouse knows when a user is erased.
CDC integration with the warehouse is a separate project. The warehouse team has their own pipelines.
Right, separate team's problem.
Out of scope.

Right. Hard rule: snapshots must use Cassandra's incremental backup with a consistent timestamp boundary. No naive nodetool snapshot on live tables. We need to verify Cassandra's incremental backup gives us what we think it does — there have been bugs there historically.
What about Amazon Neptune? Managed graph DB on AWS, supports both Gremlin and SPARQL.
Neptune at 50B edges would be expensive — their pricing is per-instance-hour and we'd need a large instance. Also vendor lock-in to AWS, which has come up in other contexts as a concern.
Both are real issues. Plus the proprietary Gremlin extensions wouldn't migrate. Not pursuing.
Neptune not pursued for cost and lock-in reasons.
Open task: verify Cassandra incremental backup behavior at our intended write rate. Not blocking the architecture decision but blocks production rollout.
And we're at 99.9% availability target, no multi-region active-active?
Right, three nines, single-region. Multi-region was discussed and explicitly deferred.
Got it.
Three nines stands.
Recorded. On the query layer: are we exposing one API or two? Different APIs per workload, or a unified Gremlin/Cypher layer over both?
I'm curious about edge weights — do we support weighted edges in the schema?
JanusGraph supports properties on edges, so weights are just a property. No special weighted-edge plumbing needed; queries that care about weights filter on the property.
Good, no special case needed.
Weights are just edge properties.
A unified layer is appealing for clients but probably impossible at our latency budget — online traversals over an analytical engine would blow the 50ms p99.
What's the on-call rotation look like for this system?
On-call structure is a people/process question. The team that builds it should own first-line.
Yeah, that's an org conversation. Park.
Parked.
Different APIs per workload. Online: a constrained REST API with a fixed set of query patterns (no ad-hoc graph traversal allowed). Analytical: full SQL via DuckDB.
Did we ever get to a decision on whether to store derived metrics (vertex degree, page-rank, etc.) in the graph itself or compute them on demand?
It got absorbed into the 'analytical layer for any non-trivial computation' principle. We don't pre-compute derived metrics in the online store; analytical handles it.
Right, online stays narrow.
Derived metrics go through analytical.
Constraining the online API is essential for the 50ms p99. Are we publishing a separate analytical Gremlin endpoint too? Some analytical workflows are easier in graph DSLs than in SQL.
I saw a paper about using LSM-tree variants tuned for graph workloads. Anyone tried that?
It's a research direction. Production-ready implementations are scarce. RocksDB-with-tuning is the closest mainstream answer.
Research-grade stuff isn't where we should be.
Mainstream LSM via Cassandra/RocksDB is enough.

No Gremlin. Just SQL on DuckDB. Gremlin adds operational complexity for negligible gain — DuckDB's SQL plus recursive CTEs covers the analytical patterns we know about, and we can revisit if a real Gremlin-only use case shows up.
Just confirming — no Redis cache in v1, right?
Right. Default-no on Redis in v1. Reconsider only if production measurements force it.
OK.
Confirmed.
Got it. So the architecture so far: writes → JanusGraph/Cassandra → consistent snapshot every 5 minutes → Parquet → DuckDB for analytical SQL. Online reads via constrained REST. Anything I'm missing?
What's the bus-factor on this whole thing? If two people get hit by buses, is the system maintainable?
Honestly, bus-factor is probably 2 right now. That's not a system property, it's an org property. We need documentation and cross-training as part of the rollout.
Add it to my mental list. Not blocking.
Noted.

Two open issues. First, how do we handle backfills of historical data — billions of new edges arrive when we onboard a new data source. Second, how do we monitor analytical-vs-online divergence to catch silent replication bugs before users notice.
What about ArangoDB? Multi-model, has good graph performance.
ArangoDB is solid but the operational story at our scale is less mature than Cassandra. We'd be relying on the multi-model promise that we don't actually need — we'd use it as a graph store, period.
Don't pay for capabilities we won't use.
ArangoDB not picked.

Backfill: a bulk load path that bypasses normal write API, writes directly to Cassandra in batches with consistent partitioning. Drains in parallel across partitions. Most graph systems have a bulk load story; JanusGraph has BulkLoaderVertexProgram.
I'm wondering about the cost of running shadow queries continuously.
Shadow queries are a tiny fraction of production traffic — every 10 minutes against a sample. Cost is negligible.
Right, negligible cost.
Shadow queries are cheap.

Bulk load bypassing the API is what I was thinking. But that creates a window where online and analytical are out of sync by more than 5 minutes during the load. Conflict with the staleness constraint.
We had a whole thread early on about using ElasticSearch as a secondary index. Why did we drop it?

ES would have given us flexible full-text search but JanusGraph composite indexes cover our enumerated query patterns. Adding ES would be a second data store to keep in sync, which violates the simpler-stack principle.
Right, sync between two stores is its own problem. ES stays off the table.
ES not pursued.
Real tension: backfill speed (hours of bulk-load throughput) vs the 5-minute staleness guarantee. During a backfill, analytical would be stale by hours, possibly days for the biggest onboardings.
Anyone want to argue for Cosmos DB with the Gremlin API?
Cosmos pricing is famously expensive at high throughput. The Gremlin API on Cosmos has some quirks vs reference Gremlin. And vendor lock-in to Azure.
Cost plus lock-in is what killed Neptune for us. Same applies to Cosmos.
Cosmos not pursued.

Open conflict. We haven't decided how to handle that. Maybe announce a known-staleness window during backfills via a status banner in the analytical UI? Don't pick now, but we need to come back to it.
Just to make sure I have it straight — the analytical staleness window is 5 minutes, right?
Yes, 5 minutes is the hard requirement from the data team. We refresh the Parquet snapshot every 5 minutes from the Cassandra source of truth.
Got it.
Confirmed.

Recorded as an open issue with a candidate resolution. Divergence monitoring: periodic shadow queries that hit both online and analytical and compare results. Diff metrics surface in monitoring, alert if they cross a threshold.
Earlier we floated using Redis with TTL-based invalidation. I want to circle back: TTL invalidation has the staleness window problem we discussed but it's operationally simple.
TTL-based invalidation is simple but you accept up to TTL seconds of staleness on cached entries. For online lookups that's hard to swallow.
Yeah, the staleness window is a problem at our latency budget. Let's drop the TTL idea. We already decided default-no on Redis anyway.
Right, that's superseded by the default-no Redis decision. Setting this aside.
Yes, shadow queries every 10 minutes covering a sample of high-traffic patterns. Diff above 0.1% triggers an alert to the on-call rotation. We log the diff for triage.
Do analytical users have a way to know if their query result is stale relative to the latest snapshot?
We expose the snapshot timestamp in the query response metadata.
OK, so transparency at the query level. Good.
Snapshot timestamp in response metadata.
Decision: shadow-query divergence monitoring, 10-minute interval, 0.1% diff threshold, alerts on-call. What about the index strategy on the online side?
Has anyone thought about how we'd handle a malformed-data ingestion incident? Bad data from upstream that corrupts a chunk of the graph.
We'd roll back to the last clean Cassandra snapshot and replay good data from the write log. Recovery time depends on how far back the corruption goes.
Not v1 priority but a real operational concern. Park it.
Parked for ops planning.

For online lookups we need predicate indexes on the most common access patterns. JanusGraph supports composite indexes; we'll need to enumerate the query patterns upfront and pick indexes accordingly. Indexes are expensive at our edge count, so we can't over-index.
Earlier draft of the rollout plan had a phased canary — 1% traffic, then 10%, then 50%. We dropped that for the cut-over approach. Why again?
Canary doesn't help when the new system is functionally different at the data layer. The data has to be identical at cut-over time; you can't canary that. Validation gates replace canary in our case.
Right. Canary is for compute-layer changes, not data-layer cut-overs.
Cut-over with validation gates stands.
Enumerating up front limits flexibility but enforces discipline. Is the trade acceptable? You're effectively saying the online API is closed-world.
Has anyone here used Memgraph? In-memory graph DB, supposedly very fast.
Memgraph is fast in-memory but the in-memory footprint at 50B edges is prohibitive. We'd need terabytes of RAM. Not realistic for our budget.
Yeah, 50B edges in RAM is out. Memgraph would be great at maybe 1B.
Memgraph shelved at our scale.
Acceptable. Locking down query patterns is part of the 50ms guarantee. We can add patterns later via a migration — not at runtime. Closed-world online API.
Have you ever seen anyone use Datalog as the query language for a system like this?
Datalog is elegant for graph reasoning but most teams find it too unfamiliar to maintain. There are systems like Datomic that use it. Not relevant to our pick though.
Just wondering. Moving on.
Sure.

Question: should we cache the online responses upstream of JanusGraph, like a Redis layer for hot keys? At 5k QPS sustained with 20k bursts, even a 30% cache hit rate would substantially reduce JanusGraph load.
What about edge-compute caching? Could we put a JanusGraph read replica at edge POPs to reduce latency for geographically distributed users?

Edge replicas for graph data are a significant operational complexity multiplier. And our users aren't geographically distributed in a way that would benefit — most traffic is from one or two regions.
Then it's overkill. Not pursuing.
Edge caching shelved.

Reflexively yes, but I want to measure first. Adding Redis adds a cache-coherence headache — when does the cache invalidate, what about stampedes after invalidation, how do we keep it in sync with writes. Don't decide on the cache yet — first measure JanusGraph's hot-path latency under load.
Cluster size is 15 nodes, static, no autoscaling?
Yes, 15 nodes sized for the 20k burst. No storage-layer autoscaling.
15 static nodes.
Confirmed.
Defer the caching decision until we have load measurements. Recorded as an open question. Concrete next step: synthetic load test against a JanusGraph cluster sized for our production target.
Are the validation gates for cut-over still all three required?
Yes: bit-exact match on holdout, 1.5x throughput, 7-day soak. All three are hard gates.
OK, three hard gates.
Three hard gates confirmed.

Coming back to consistency: we said snapshots must be transactionally consistent. Cassandra's incremental backups give us a timestamp boundary but not a snapshot isolation in the SQL sense. Some rows may reflect in-flight transactions. Is that a problem for analytical?
Could we use bloom filters somewhere to short-circuit existence queries?
JanusGraph already uses bloom filters in its Cassandra storage layer. They're transparent — we benefit without doing anything specific.
Good, already covered.
Bloom filters are in there by default.

For most analytical queries — aggregations, descriptive stats, ranking — eventually consistent is fine. For audit-style queries that need exact totals at a given timestamp, it's a problem because the snapshot may include some half-committed work.
Have we costed out a chaos-engineering practice? Periodic node kills, network partitions, etc.
Chaos engineering is best-practice but adds engineering load. For v1 we'd probably stop at game-day exercises and add chaos-engineering tooling later.
Game-days first, chaos automation later. Fine.
Agreed.

We don't have audit-style analytical queries in scope right now. Treat eventual consistency as acceptable for v1. If audit comes up later, we revisit — but I'd rather not bake in snapshot isolation as a constraint when we don't need it.
Just confirming the 14-day daily plus 12-month monthly retention?
Right. 14 days at daily granularity, then monthly snapshots out to 12 months.
Got it.
Retention policy stands.
Eventual consistency for analytical accepted for v1. Audit-style queries explicitly out of scope until later. What about schema evolution — adding a new edge type or property?
Do we have a story for graceful degradation if Cassandra has a partial outage?

Cassandra with RF=3 tolerates one node down per replication group transparently. Beyond that, we'd be serving stale/partial data. Graceful degradation policy isn't formalized — we'd need to decide whether to fail open or fail closed.
Another formalization gap. Park it.
Parked.

Schema evolution must be backward-compatible. New edge types can be added freely; new optional properties on existing types can be added freely. Existing edge types cannot be renamed or have their property schemas tightened without a migration.
There was an earlier proposal to use Kafka as the write log so we could replay. We went with something else, right?
Cassandra's commit log effectively serves as our write log via the incremental backup mechanism. Adding Kafka would be a parallel system for the same purpose.
Yeah, don't duplicate. Cassandra's log is enough.
Kafka not added.
Hard rule: schema changes are additive-only at the edge-type level. Renames or tightenings require migrations with a planned downtime window or a dual-write period.
Anyone thought about how this system would migrate if we changed cloud providers?
Cassandra and DuckDB are portable. JanusGraph is portable. The architecture is largely cloud-agnostic by design.
Good, that wasn't accidental.
Cloud-agnostic by design.
Yes. And migrations must be replayable — given a baseline state, applying the migration twice must be a no-op. Otherwise rollback during a partial failure becomes a nightmare.
At some point we floated the idea of a write-through cache that synchronously writes to both Cassandra and Redis. Why didn't that fly?
Write-through means every write pays double latency and you have a coherence problem if one side fails. Plus we now have default-no on Redis anyway.
Right, both problems. Dropped.
Write-through cache shelved.
Migrations are idempotent. Recorded as a constraint. That also implies migrations need a versioned migration log so we can tell what has run.
I keep wondering about disaster-recovery RTO/RPO targets. Have we written those down?
We haven't actually written formal DR targets. The implicit assumption is the Cassandra RF=3 plus object-storage backups give us 'good enough' DR, but we haven't measured.
We should formalize that eventually. Adding to my mental list, not blocking anything.
Noted, not actioned.
Right, versioned migration log, with the version stored in the graph itself so a recovered backup carries its own version. Implementation detail but important.
Just to verify — Spark is out, right?
Right, Spark is dropped. DuckDB handles our analytical layer.
OK.
Confirmed.
Implementation detail noted. About the JanusGraph choice — I want to verify the operational story. Who at our org has run JanusGraph at scale? If nobody, we have a hidden cost.
Hugegraph? Another open-source Chinese option.
Hugegraph is interesting but smaller community than JanusGraph and less English documentation.
Same answer as Nebula. Not pursuing.
Hugegraph not pursued.

Honest question. I don't think anyone has. We have one engineer who ran a small JanusGraph cluster at her previous job, but nothing at our scale and not recently. Open question for the platform team — do we have operational expertise, or do we need to hire it?
Going back to the schema-versioning discussion: I had drafted a proposal where every vertex carries a schema version. We did something simpler in the end?
Per-vertex schema version is heavyweight. We landed on a single global migration log version stored in a control vertex.
Right, additive rule means we don't need per-vertex versions.
Global migration version stands.
If we need to hire JanusGraph ops expertise, that's a 6-month timeline minimum given the market. And it gates production rollout.
I remember we briefly considered ZeroMQ for the ingestion-to-storage path. Why isn't that in the final picture?
ZeroMQ is excellent at low-latency message passing but doesn't give us durability. We need durable queuing so we landed on a managed durable queue with at-least-once semantics.
Right, we need durability. ZeroMQ stays out.
ZMQ rejected on durability grounds.

Open question, important. Don't commit to JanusGraph for production until we know whether we can staff it. The architecture decision stands, but the production rollout is conditional on having the ops capability.
OrientDB? It's been around forever, multi-model like Arango.
OrientDB's been in maintenance-mode for years. The community has thinned. Not a safe bet for new infrastructure.
Right, hard pass.
OrientDB rejected on ecosystem health.
Conditional commitment: JanusGraph is the architecture choice, but production rollout requires confirmed ops expertise. Otherwise we reconsider the whole online side.
Have we thought about what happens if Cassandra has a poison-pill query that takes down a node?
Cassandra has some query-killer capabilities but they're imperfect. We'd want to add query monitoring at the JanusGraph layer to catch pathological queries before they hit Cassandra.
Probably an ops concern more than design-time. Park it.
Parked.

Also a tension I want to record: we've been picking technologies based on scale fit, but the team is small. The more exotic our stack, the higher the operational tax. JanusGraph + Cassandra + DuckDB + Parquet is four moving parts at minimum, plus whatever caching layer we add later. There's a real tension between picking-the-right-tool and minimizing-the-stack.
If a partner contract changes mid-quarter, how fast can we update their scope?
View definitions are quick to update. New scope is live within hours.
Hours for live scope changes. Good.
Fast partner-scope changes possible.

Real tension, worth surfacing. We haven't actually weighed it against the architecture. Maybe a simpler stack — say, Postgres with the AGE graph extension — would be wrong on scale but right on operational load. Worth a sanity check before we commit.
What about RedisGraph? Redis with a graph module on top, all in-memory.
RedisGraph is fast in-memory but Redis itself was abandoned by Redis Labs for graph use cases — they shut it down in 2023. So it's not a live option.
Oh, didn't know it was discontinued. Hard pass then.
RedisGraph dead. Not pursued.

Yes. Don't change the architecture, but do the sanity check: would Postgres-with-AGE actually fail at our scale, or only feel risky? If it would actually fail, we keep the current direction. If it would work but be slower than we like, we have a decision to make.
Has anyone here used Dgraph in production?
Dgraph has interesting GraphQL-native design. Operational reports are mixed at scale. The company had instability issues in 2022-2023 which affects long-term confidence.
Vendor stability is a real concern. Not safe to bet on for new infra.
Dgraph not pursued on stability grounds.
Sanity check on Postgres-with-AGE as an alternative. Open task. Some quick numbers: AGE inherits Postgres's per-table size limits which we'd hit; sharding would require Citus, which adds complexity.
Early in the discussion we talked about exposing a streaming API for real-time updates to analytical clients. We dropped that, right?
Real-time analytical updates is on the explicitly-deferred list (D26). Clients accept the 5-minute snapshot window.
Right, that's post-v1.
Streaming API deferred.
Citus-on-AGE is getting exotic again. That's exactly what we said we wanted to avoid. If AGE alone won't fit and the sharded version is exotic, that closes the door.
Have we considered offering a GraphQL gateway in front of the REST API for clients who want declarative queries?
GraphQL on top of our constrained REST would be a thin shim. It would add a layer to operate without adding new capabilities. Most clients are internal services that already use REST.
Not worth it then.
GraphQL not pursued.
So Postgres-with-AGE is likely a no-go on scale grounds. Direction is preserved — JanusGraph stays the architecture pick.
What if we open-sourced the parts of our system that aren't customer-specific?
Open-sourcing is a legal and product decision more than a technical one. Not in scope of this design conversation.
Right, that's an exec call. Not here.
Out of scope.

Last thing for today on the architecture: there was an earlier idea to use Apache Spark for the analytical layer, before we landed on DuckDB. That direction is dropped — Spark's operational complexity is high and we don't need its distributed compute for our analytical volumes, which fit on a single fat node.
If we have to do a full data re-ingest from upstream, what's the timeline?
At our bulk-load throughput, re-ingesting 50B edges would take days.
Days. Good to know.
Days bound recorded informally.
Spark direction dropped. DuckDB stands as the analytical engine. Are we ready to move to security and compliance now?
What about temporal queries — 'show me the graph as it was a week ago'?

Temporal queries on a graph that doesn't track per-vertex history would require expensive reconstruction from snapshots. We're not building that in v1; daily snapshots give partial time-travel for analytical.
Daily snapshots are enough for now.
Temporal queries via snapshot history. No first-class temporal.
Yes. We have customer data in this graph, so this is real. We need to be GDPR-compliant on the analytical side and on the online side.
Are we still going with JanusGraph or did we revisit that?
Still JanusGraph. The decision stood through the various sanity checks — Postgres-with-AGE wouldn't scale, Neo4j cost was too high. Production rollout is conditional on operational expertise.
OK, still JanusGraph then.
Yes.

GDPR has a few load-bearing requirements: right to access, right to erasure, data minimization, lawful basis. Erasure is the hard one for a graph — when a user requests deletion, we need to remove their vertices and edges from both online and analytical.
Going back to the snapshot frequency question — at one point I was thinking 1-minute snapshots for tighter freshness. Why did we land on 5 minutes again?
1-minute snapshots produce ~5x the snapshot volume. Storage cost and snapshot pipeline load become problematic. 5 minutes was the compromise the data team accepted.
Right. 1-minute would have been nicer but the data team OK'd 5. We're not revisiting that.
Five minutes stands.
Erasure is the hard one. We need to track which subgraphs belong to which user. Vertex-level user-id labelling won't be enough because edges between users encode information about both.
Random thought: what if we used a graph neural network to predict hot paths and pre-warm the cache?

ML-based cache warming exists in research papers but in practice the deterministic 'most-recently-used' or 'most-frequently-used' heuristics get you 90% of the way. ML adds operational complexity for marginal gain.
Yeah, not for v1. Maybe v3 if we have the data to train it.
Deferred indefinitely.

Right. Edge ownership is a real issue: an edge between users A and B contains information about both. If A requests erasure, what happens to the edge? Strictly, you delete it. But that affects B's data.
Do we have a plan for what happens when JanusGraph releases a major version update?
Major version updates of a production data system are always painful. We'd do a parallel cluster with replay-based migration, similar to our initial cut-over plan.
OK, but we don't need to plan that for v1. It's a future-team problem.
Future problem.

Constraint: when a user requests erasure, all vertices solely owned by that user are hard-deleted; all edges where that user is an endpoint are hard-deleted; the deletion is propagated to the analytical layer within the next snapshot window.
Random thought: what if we used homomorphic encryption so the cloud provider can't see our graph data even if they wanted to?
Homomorphic encryption for graph operations is hundreds of times slower than plaintext. Latency budget would be destroyed.
Yeah, the latency tradeoff makes it a non-starter.
HE shelved.
Hard rule: erasure deletes user's vertices and user's edges in both layers, with analytical propagation within one snapshot window (5 minutes).
Do we have any sense of what happens if our partner Alpha onboards 10x the queries we expected?
Their rate limit at the gateway would kick in. We'd see lots of 429s on their end and not be impacted ourselves.
Right, technical answer is fine. Contract is a separate conversation.
Tech side handled by rate limits.
And we need an erasure log — a record that the erasure happened, separate from the deleted data, for audit purposes. The log is keyed by user id and event timestamp.
Just want to double-check the cut-over plan — replay-based migration, not dual-write?
Replay-based from a known checkpoint. Dual-write was rejected because it would double write load.
Right.
Replay it is.
Erasure log decision: keep an append-only erasure log keyed by user id and timestamp, separate from the deleted data. This itself raises a meta-question: does the erasure log count as user data?
And no Friday cut-overs, no pre-holiday, no PTO?
Right, all three scheduling constraints. They're a hard rule.
Got it.
Scheduling rule confirmed.

Hmm. Legally, an erasure log keyed by user id IS user data — but it's the bare minimum required to prove the erasure happened, which is itself a GDPR obligation. There's a tension between right-to-erasure and right-to-audit. Open issue, escalate to legal.
Earlier we discussed CRDT-based conflict resolution for cross-region writes. Did we ever resolve that?
It got absorbed into the 'no multi-region active-active for v1' decision. If we ever go active-active, CRDT design becomes a real conversation.
OK so CRDT is post-v1 territory.
Tied to active-active decision. Post-v1.
Open issue: legal interpretation of the erasure log under GDPR. Don't ship without an answer.
What about NebulaGraph? It's open source from China, allegedly scales well.
NebulaGraph has good benchmarks but the documentation outside Chinese is patchy. Operational support for an English-speaking team would be a challenge.
Documentation matters. JanusGraph stays.
Nebula not pursued.

Right. Also: we agreed earlier that snapshots are eventually consistent for analytical. After an erasure, the analytical snapshot may still contain the deleted user for up to 5 minutes. Is that OK under GDPR?
What if we built a query plan analyzer that warns developers about expensive queries before they ship?
Static query analysis on Gremlin/Cypher is hard because the cost depends on data shapes. Best-effort tools exist but they're imperfect.
Sounds like nice-to-have but not essential.
Nice-to-have, post-v1.
GDPR doesn't require instant erasure; it requires erasure 'without undue delay'. 5 minutes is fine. But we should document this clearly.
GDPR erasure still propagates within one snapshot window?
Yes, 5 minutes worst-case for analytical. Online is immediate.
5 minutes for analytical, immediate for online.
Confirmed.
Document the 5-minute analytical erasure window as a known property of the system. Make sure the data team's analytical users understand it — they're querying data that may include just-erased users.
I had an earlier idea about using ScyllaDB instead of Cassandra. Same wire protocol, better per-node throughput. Why didn't we pick it?
Operational maturity. Cassandra is well-understood; Scylla is a smaller ecosystem and we'd be the first in the org to run it. The throughput edge wasn't worth the unknown.
Right. We default to the more boring choice when capabilities are similar.
Cassandra it is. Scylla shelved indefinitely.
Documentation requirement: 5-minute analytical erasure window must be visible to analytical users via the API and UI.
What about AllegroGraph? Mature semantic-web graph DB.
AllegroGraph is RDF/SPARQL-focused like Stardog. Different problem domain. We're property-graph, not semantic-web.
Right, wrong tool.
AllegroGraph not relevant.
Moving to monitoring: we need SLO definitions. p99 online latency is the obvious one. What else?
Out of curiosity: what would the system look like if we wrote it in Rust instead of using the JVM-based JanusGraph?
Rust would mean writing our own graph engine. The engineering cost is enormous — we'd be a graph-database company instead of a customer-facing analytics company. Not viable.
Yeah, that was just curiosity. Not a real proposal.
Right. JanusGraph stays.
Beyond p99 latency: error rate (4xx and 5xx separately), saturation (queue depth, JVM memory in JanusGraph), and the freshness SLO — the time-since-last-successful-snapshot for analytical.
How does our setup compare to Stardog, Ontotext, or other commercial graph platforms? Anything we're missing?

Stardog and Ontotext target the semantic-web / RDF / SPARQL world. Strong on inference and reasoning, weaker on the raw-edge-count throughput we need. Our property-graph workload doesn't benefit from their semantic strengths.
So they're solving a different problem. Not applicable.
Different problem domain. No action.
SLOs: p99 online latency under 50ms, error rate under 0.1%, JanusGraph queue depth under 100, freshness under 6 minutes (gives 1 minute headroom over the 5-minute target).
And we're not adding Gremlin to the analytical side?
Correct. SQL only on analytical. Gremlin not pursued.
Right.
SQL-only on analytical.
SLO list recorded. The freshness SLO is interesting — if it's breached, what's the action? Page someone, or just alert?
And online lookups have to be under 50ms at p99?
Yes, p99 under 50ms, p50 under 10ms, sustained at 5k QPS with bursts to 20k.
Right.
That's the latency budget.
Alert below 6 minutes, page above 10. Below 10 we have time to investigate before users notice. Above 10 we're at risk of user-visible staleness, page immediately.
Going back to authentication: we considered using API keys instead of OAuth for service-to-service. Why did we pick mTLS in the end?

API keys are bearer tokens — anyone with the key has access. mTLS ties identity to a certificate that's harder to exfiltrate and easier to rotate. For service-to-service inside our infrastructure, mTLS is the higher bar.
Yeah, mTLS for service-to-service is correct. API keys would be a regression.
mTLS for the ingestion service stays. API keys are not on the table.
Page threshold for freshness: 10 minutes. Alert threshold: 6 minutes. Recorded.
Are we still encrypting both at rest and in transit?
Yes, both. Cassandra and Parquet at rest, all client connections in transit. KMS for keys.
OK.
Encryption confirmed.
On to rollout. We have a small staging environment with synthetic data. Production data is 50B edges. We need a rollout plan that doesn't risk corrupting production.
Random idea: integrate with our user-data warehouse via change-data-capture so the warehouse knows when a user is erased.
CDC integration with the warehouse is a separate project. The warehouse team has their own pipelines.
Right, separate team's problem.
Out of scope.

Standard playbook: build the new system alongside the old, dual-write for a period, validate the new system against the old, then cut over. Cut-over is one-way; we don't try to roll back to a stale old system.
What about Amazon Neptune? Managed graph DB on AWS, supports both Gremlin and SPARQL.
Neptune at 50B edges would be expensive — their pricing is per-instance-hour and we'd need a large instance. Also vendor lock-in to AWS, which has come up in other contexts as a concern.
Both are real issues. Plus the proprietary Gremlin extensions wouldn't migrate. Not pursuing.
Neptune not pursued for cost and lock-in reasons.
Dual-write is risky at our scale — we'd be doubling write load. Can we instead replay the write log into the new system from a known checkpoint, validate, and cut over without dual-writing?
And we're at 99.9% availability target, no multi-region active-active?
Right, three nines, single-region. Multi-region was discussed and explicitly deferred.
Got it.
Three nines stands.
Replay-based migration works if our write log is durable and replayable from a checkpoint. Faster but riskier — any bug in the replay path becomes invisible until the cut-over.
I'm curious about edge weights — do we support weighted edges in the schema?
JanusGraph supports properties on edges, so weights are just a property. No special weighted-edge plumbing needed; queries that care about weights filter on the property.
Good, no special case needed.
Weights are just edge properties.

Decision: replay-based migration, not dual-write. We can afford to spend more time validating before cut-over because we don't have a hard launch date. Slowness is acceptable; doubling write load is not.
What's the on-call rotation look like for this system?
On-call structure is a people/process question. The team that builds it should own first-line.
Yeah, that's an org conversation. Park.
Parked.

Replay-based migration chosen, dual-write rejected. Validation must include: bit-exact comparison of query results between old and new on a holdout query set, throughput tests on the new system at 1.5x peak production load, and a 7-day soak in staging.
Did we ever get to a decision on whether to store derived metrics (vertex degree, page-rank, etc.) in the graph itself or compute them on demand?
It got absorbed into the 'analytical layer for any non-trivial computation' principle. We don't pre-compute derived metrics in the online store; analytical handles it.
Right, online stays narrow.
Derived metrics go through analytical.
Validation gates: bit-exact result match on holdout, 1.5x throughput test passes, 7-day soak with no errors. All three must pass before cut-over.
I saw a paper about using LSM-tree variants tuned for graph workloads. Anyone tried that?
It's a research direction. Production-ready implementations are scarce. RocksDB-with-tuning is the closest mainstream answer.
Research-grade stuff isn't where we should be.
Mainstream LSM via Cassandra/RocksDB is enough.
All three are hard gates. Recorded.
Just confirming — no Redis cache in v1, right?
Right. Default-no on Redis in v1. Reconsider only if production measurements force it.
OK.
Confirmed.

I want to also document a constraint I haven't said explicitly: no cut-over on a Friday, no cut-over before a holiday, no cut-over while any team member is on PTO. Boring rule but it's saved us before.
What's the bus-factor on this whole thing? If two people get hit by buses, is the system maintainable?
Honestly, bus-factor is probably 2 right now. That's not a system property, it's an org property. We need documentation and cross-training as part of the rollout.
Add it to my mental list. Not blocking.
Noted.
Cut-over scheduling constraint: no Friday, no pre-holiday, no PTO of any team member. Recorded as a hard rule.
What about ArangoDB? Multi-model, has good graph performance.
ArangoDB is solid but the operational story at our scale is less mature than Cassandra. We'd be relying on the multi-model promise that we don't actually need — we'd use it as a graph store, period.
Don't pay for capabilities we won't use.
ArangoDB not picked.

One more thing. We talked about Redis caching as an open question earlier. I want to revisit: even before measuring, should we just say no to Redis as a first-pass policy, and only add it if measurements show we need it? Adding now is a footgun if we don't actually need it.
I'm wondering about the cost of running shadow queries continuously.
Shadow queries are a tiny fraction of production traffic — every 10 minutes against a sample. Cost is negligible.
Right, negligible cost.
Shadow queries are cheap.
There's an argument for a default-no policy on Redis. Adding caches without need is a common source of bugs and cache-coherence headaches.
We had a whole thread early on about using ElasticSearch as a secondary index. Why did we drop it?

ES would have given us flexible full-text search but JanusGraph composite indexes cover our enumerated query patterns. Adding ES would be a second data store to keep in sync, which violates the simpler-stack principle.
Right, sync between two stores is its own problem. ES stays off the table.
ES not pursued.

Yes. Update the earlier open question to a default-no decision: no Redis cache in v1. Reconsider only if measurements show JanusGraph hot-path can't hit 50ms p99 without help. That replaces the earlier 'defer until measurement' position.
Anyone want to argue for Cosmos DB with the Gremlin API?
Cosmos pricing is famously expensive at high throughput. The Gremlin API on Cosmos has some quirks vs reference Gremlin. And vendor lock-in to Azure.
Cost plus lock-in is what killed Neptune for us. Same applies to Cosmos.
Cosmos not pursued.
Updated decision: no Redis cache in v1. Reconsider only if production measurements force it. This replaces the earlier defer-until-measurement decision on caching.
Just to make sure I have it straight — the analytical staleness window is 5 minutes, right?
Yes, 5 minutes is the hard requirement from the data team. We refresh the Parquet snapshot every 5 minutes from the Cassandra source of truth.
Got it.
Confirmed.
Coming back to security: we should think about access control. Who can write to the online store? Who can read the analytical store?
Earlier we floated using Redis with TTL-based invalidation. I want to circle back: TTL invalidation has the staleness window problem we discussed but it's operationally simple.
TTL-based invalidation is simple but you accept up to TTL seconds of staleness on cached entries. For online lookups that's hard to swallow.
Yeah, the staleness window is a problem at our latency budget. Let's drop the TTL idea. We already decided default-no on Redis anyway.
Right, that's superseded by the default-no Redis decision. Setting this aside.

Writes to online: only the ingestion service, authenticated via mTLS. Reads from online: any authenticated service, but the REST API enforces row-level access based on user identity. Reads from analytical: data team users, authenticated via SSO.
Do analytical users have a way to know if their query result is stale relative to the latest snapshot?
We expose the snapshot timestamp in the query response metadata.
OK, so transparency at the query level. Good.
Snapshot timestamp in response metadata.
Access control: writes to online restricted to ingestion service via mTLS; online reads via authenticated REST with row-level access; analytical reads via SSO to data team only.
Has anyone thought about how we'd handle a malformed-data ingestion incident? Bad data from upstream that corrupts a chunk of the graph.
We'd roll back to the last clean Cassandra snapshot and replay good data from the write log. Recovery time depends on how far back the corruption goes.
Not v1 priority but a real operational concern. Park it.
Parked for ops planning.
Recorded. Encryption at rest and in transit?
Earlier draft of the rollout plan had a phased canary — 1% traffic, then 10%, then 50%. We dropped that for the cut-over approach. Why again?
Canary doesn't help when the new system is functionally different at the data layer. The data has to be identical at cut-over time; you can't canary that. Validation gates replace canary in our case.
Right. Canary is for compute-layer changes, not data-layer cut-overs.
Cut-over with validation gates stands.
Encryption at rest for both Cassandra and the Parquet snapshot files. Encryption in transit for all client connections. No exceptions. We use the standard org KMS for keys.
Has anyone here used Memgraph? In-memory graph DB, supposedly very fast.
Memgraph is fast in-memory but the in-memory footprint at 50B edges is prohibitive. We'd need terabytes of RAM. Not realistic for our budget.
Yeah, 50B edges in RAM is out. Memgraph would be great at maybe 1B.
Memgraph shelved at our scale.
Constraint: encryption at rest for Cassandra and Parquet; encryption in transit for all client connections; key management via standard org KMS.
Have you ever seen anyone use Datalog as the query language for a system like this?
Datalog is elegant for graph reasoning but most teams find it too unfamiliar to maintain. There are systems like Datomic that use it. Not relevant to our pick though.
Just wondering. Moving on.
Sure.
OK that covers the main shape. What did we leave undecided?
What about edge-compute caching? Could we put a JanusGraph read replica at edge POPs to reduce latency for geographically distributed users?

Edge replicas for graph data are a significant operational complexity multiplier. And our users aren't geographically distributed in a way that would benefit — most traffic is from one or two regions.
Then it's overkill. Not pursuing.
Edge caching shelved.

Open issues: backfill-vs-staleness conflict (no resolution), JanusGraph ops capability (open to platform team), erasure log legal status (open to legal), Cassandra incremental backup verification (open task before production).
Cluster size is 15 nodes, static, no autoscaling?
Yes, 15 nodes sized for the 20k burst. No storage-layer autoscaling.
15 static nodes.
Confirmed.
Good summary. These are the gating items before any production commitment. Let's reconvene next week with answers on at least the ops capability question.
Are the validation gates for cut-over still all three required?
Yes: bit-exact match on holdout, 1.5x throughput, 7-day soak. All three are hard gates.
OK, three hard gates.
Three hard gates confirmed.
Actually before we wrap, let's talk performance and capacity. The 50ms p99 on online — what's our actual budget breakdown? Network, query engine, serialization?
Could we use bloom filters somewhere to short-circuit existence queries?
JanusGraph already uses bloom filters in its Cassandra storage layer. They're transparent — we benefit without doing anything specific.
Good, already covered.
Bloom filters are in there by default.

Rough budget for a single online request at 50ms p99: 5ms client-to-LB, 5ms LB-to-app, 25ms in JanusGraph (the dominant cost), 5ms serialization, 5ms response path. JanusGraph getting 25ms is tight for multi-hop traversals.
Have we costed out a chaos-engineering practice? Periodic node kills, network partitions, etc.
Chaos engineering is best-practice but adds engineering load. For v1 we'd probably stop at game-day exercises and add chaos-engineering tooling later.
Game-days first, chaos automation later. Fine.
Agreed.

25ms for JanusGraph is the binding constraint. What if our queries average two hops with a property filter at each hop? That's roughly two indexed lookups plus an edge expansion. Can JanusGraph hit that in 25ms at the 99th percentile?
Just confirming the 14-day daily plus 12-month monthly retention?
Right. 14 days at daily granularity, then monthly snapshots out to 12 months.
Got it.
Retention policy stands.

On a hot cache, two-hop indexed traversals at JanusGraph have been reported in the low-tens-of-milliseconds range. On a cold cache it's much worse. So the 25ms p99 is feasible only if cache hit rates stay high.
Do we have a story for graceful degradation if Cassandra has a partial outage?

Cassandra with RF=3 tolerates one node down per replication group transparently. Beyond that, we'd be serving stale/partial data. Graceful degradation policy isn't formalized — we'd need to decide whether to fail open or fail closed.
Another formalization gap. Park it.
Parked.
That's a real risk. Open question: what's our minimum acceptable JanusGraph cache hit rate to maintain the 50ms p99? If we can quantify it, we can size memory accordingly.
There was an earlier proposal to use Kafka as the write log so we could replay. We went with something else, right?
Cassandra's commit log effectively serves as our write log via the incremental backup mechanism. Adding Kafka would be a parallel system for the same purpose.
Yeah, don't duplicate. Cassandra's log is enough.
Kafka not added.
Open task: derive minimum cache hit rate from the latency budget. Probably north of 85%, but we need to measure. This also informs the Redis question we deferred.
Anyone thought about how this system would migrate if we changed cloud providers?
Cassandra and DuckDB are portable. JanusGraph is portable. The architecture is largely cloud-agnostic by design.
Good, that wasn't accidental.
Cloud-agnostic by design.
On capacity sizing: at 5k QPS sustained, what does the JanusGraph cluster look like? Each node serves maybe 1k QPS at our latency target?
At some point we floated the idea of a write-through cache that synchronously writes to both Cassandra and Redis. Why didn't that fly?
Write-through means every write pays double latency and you have a coherence problem if one side fails. Plus we now have default-no on Redis anyway.
Right, both problems. Dropped.
Write-through cache shelved.

Public benchmarks suggest a beefy JanusGraph node (32 cores, 128GB) handles 500-2000 QPS for low-complexity traversals, depending heavily on hot-set fit in memory. So you're looking at 5-10 nodes for steady state, more for burst headroom.
I keep wondering about disaster-recovery RTO/RPO targets. Have we written those down?
We haven't actually written formal DR targets. The implicit assumption is the Cassandra RF=3 plus object-storage backups give us 'good enough' DR, but we haven't measured.
We should formalize that eventually. Adding to my mental list, not blocking anything.
Noted, not actioned.

Plan for 8 nodes steady state, headroom up to 15 for the 20k bursts. We need autoscaling on the JanusGraph layer — but JanusGraph nodes aren't stateless, so autoscaling means adding nodes to the cluster, which involves data redistribution. That's not fast.
Just to verify — Spark is out, right?
Right, Spark is dropped. DuckDB handles our analytical layer.
OK.
Confirmed.

JanusGraph autoscaling is constrained by Cassandra's rebalancing cost. We can't scale up in seconds; new nodes take minutes to hours to be useful, depending on the data partition strategy. So we need to overprovision for bursts rather than try to scale into them.
Hugegraph? Another open-source Chinese option.
Hugegraph is interesting but smaller community than JanusGraph and less English documentation.
Same answer as Nebula. Not pursuing.
Hugegraph not pursued.
Decision: overprovision for the 20k burst. Static cluster of 15 nodes. Cost goes up but we avoid the complexity of autoscaling at the storage layer.
Going back to the schema-versioning discussion: I had drafted a proposal where every vertex carries a schema version. We did something simpler in the end?
Per-vertex schema version is heavyweight. We landed on a single global migration log version stored in a control vertex.
Right, additive rule means we don't need per-vertex versions.
Global migration version stands.
Recorded: static 15-node JanusGraph cluster sized for 20k peak. No storage-layer autoscaling.
I remember we briefly considered ZeroMQ for the ingestion-to-storage path. Why isn't that in the final picture?
ZeroMQ is excellent at low-latency message passing but doesn't give us durability. We need durable queuing so we landed on a managed durable queue with at-least-once semantics.
Right, we need durability. ZeroMQ stays out.
ZMQ rejected on durability grounds.
On the Cassandra side — what's the storage footprint at 50B edges?
OrientDB? It's been around forever, multi-model like Arango.
OrientDB's been in maintenance-mode for years. The community has thinned. Not a safe bet for new infrastructure.
Right, hard pass.
OrientDB rejected on ecosystem health.

Average JanusGraph edge in Cassandra is around 200-400 bytes including indexing overhead. 50B edges at 300 bytes = 15TB raw. With replication factor 3 (standard for Cassandra durability), that's 45TB. Plus vertex storage, plus secondary indexes, you're at 50-60TB across the cluster.
Have we thought about what happens if Cassandra has a poison-pill query that takes down a node?
Cassandra has some query-killer capabilities but they're imperfect. We'd want to add query monitoring at the JanusGraph layer to catch pathological queries before they hit Cassandra.
Probably an ops concern more than design-time. Park it.
Parked.
50-60TB is a lot of SSD. Are we using local NVMe or network-attached? Local is fast but loses with node failures; network-attached is slower but survives node loss.
If a partner contract changes mid-quarter, how fast can we update their scope?
View definitions are quick to update. New scope is live within hours.
Hours for live scope changes. Good.
Fast partner-scope changes possible.

For Cassandra at this scale, local NVMe with replication factor 3 is the standard answer. Network-attached storage adds latency that hits the 25ms JanusGraph budget hard. Lose a node, you re-replicate from the other two replicas.
What about RedisGraph? Redis with a graph module on top, all in-memory.
RedisGraph is fast in-memory but Redis itself was abandoned by Redis Labs for graph use cases — they shut it down in 2023. So it's not a live option.
Oh, didn't know it was discontinued. Hard pass then.
RedisGraph dead. Not pursued.
Local NVMe, RF=3. Recorded. Backup is separate — incremental backup goes to object storage (S3 or equivalent).
Has anyone here used Dgraph in production?
Dgraph has interesting GraphQL-native design. Operational reports are mixed at scale. The company had instability issues in 2022-2023 which affects long-term confidence.
Vendor stability is a real concern. Not safe to bet on for new infra.
Dgraph not pursued on stability grounds.
Storage: local NVMe for live Cassandra data, RF=3; incremental backups streamed to object storage. Recorded.
Early in the discussion we talked about exposing a streaming API for real-time updates to analytical clients. We dropped that, right?
Real-time analytical updates is on the explicitly-deferred list (D26). Clients accept the 5-minute snapshot window.
Right, that's post-v1.
Streaming API deferred.
How big is the analytical Parquet snapshot? If the live data is 15TB, the Parquet form should be smaller due to columnar compression.
Have we considered offering a GraphQL gateway in front of the REST API for clients who want declarative queries?
GraphQL on top of our constrained REST would be a thin shim. It would add a layer to operate without adding new capabilities. Most clients are internal services that already use REST.
Not worth it then.
GraphQL not pursued.

Parquet on graph data typically gets 3-5x compression depending on how the edge properties tile. So maybe 3-5TB per snapshot. We keep one current snapshot plus N historical snapshots for analytical history.
What if we open-sourced the parts of our system that aren't customer-specific?
Open-sourcing is a legal and product decision more than a technical one. Not in scope of this design conversation.
Right, that's an exec call. Not here.
Out of scope.
Question: how many historical snapshots do we keep? Each costs storage but enables time-travel queries on the analytical side.
If we have to do a full data re-ingest from upstream, what's the timeline?
At our bulk-load throughput, re-ingesting 50B edges would take days.
Days. Good to know.
Days bound recorded informally.

Default policy proposal: keep the last 14 daily snapshots plus monthly snapshots going back 12 months. That's 14 + 12 = 26 snapshots at 3-5TB each = 80-130TB in object storage. Cheap compared to live storage but not free.
What about temporal queries — 'show me the graph as it was a week ago'?

Temporal queries on a graph that doesn't track per-vertex history would require expensive reconstruction from snapshots. We're not building that in v1; daily snapshots give partial time-travel for analytical.
Daily snapshots are enough for now.
Temporal queries via snapshot history. No first-class temporal.
14 days hot, 12 months at monthly. That's a good baseline. The 5-minute snapshots we use for fresh analytical reads are not retained beyond the current one; we only retain dailies for history.
Are we still going with JanusGraph or did we revisit that?
Still JanusGraph. The decision stood through the various sanity checks — Postgres-with-AGE wouldn't scale, Neo4j cost was too high. Production rollout is conditional on operational expertise.
OK, still JanusGraph then.
Yes.
Retention decision: 5-minute snapshots retained only as the current one (no history); daily snapshots retained 14 days; monthly snapshots retained 12 months. Recorded.
Going back to the snapshot frequency question — at one point I was thinking 1-minute snapshots for tighter freshness. Why did we land on 5 minutes again?
1-minute snapshots produce ~5x the snapshot volume. Storage cost and snapshot pipeline load become problematic. 5 minutes was the compromise the data team accepted.
Right. 1-minute would have been nicer but the data team OK'd 5. We're not revisiting that.
Five minutes stands.
Cost ballpark for the full setup?
Random thought: what if we used a graph neural network to predict hot paths and pre-warm the cache?

ML-based cache warming exists in research papers but in practice the deterministic 'most-recently-used' or 'most-frequently-used' heuristics get you 90% of the way. ML adds operational complexity for marginal gain.
Yeah, not for v1. Maybe v3 if we have the data to train it.
Deferred indefinitely.

Order-of-magnitude estimate: 15 JanusGraph/Cassandra nodes at $1.5k/month each = $22.5k/month compute. 60TB local NVMe in cloud is roughly $6k/month. Object storage for backups and analytical snapshots: 130TB at $20/TB/month = $2.6k. DuckDB analytical node: $2k/month for a beefy single instance. Total: roughly $33k/month, $400k/year, before networking and observability.
Do we have a plan for what happens when JanusGraph releases a major version update?
Major version updates of a production data system are always painful. We'd do a parallel cluster with replay-based migration, similar to our initial cut-over plan.
OK, but we don't need to plan that for v1. It's a future-team problem.
Future problem.

$400k/year is a real number. We need to defend it. What does our current solution cost? If the current system is $300k/year and this is $400k/year for capabilities we actually need, that's defensible.
Random thought: what if we used homomorphic encryption so the cloud provider can't see our graph data even if they wanted to?
Homomorphic encryption for graph operations is hundreds of times slower than plaintext. Latency budget would be destroyed.
Yeah, the latency tradeoff makes it a non-starter.
HE shelved.
Open question: current annual cost of the legacy graph system. We need this for the cost-justification conversation with leadership.
Do we have any sense of what happens if our partner Alpha onboards 10x the queries we expected?
Their rate limit at the gateway would kick in. We'd see lots of 429s on their end and not be impacted ourselves.
Right, technical answer is fine. Contract is a separate conversation.
Tech side handled by rate limits.

Right. And we should think about cost optimization paths from day 1. The biggest lever is probably the JanusGraph node count, which is driven by burst capacity. If we can compress bursts via rate limiting at the API gateway, we can reduce node count.
Just want to double-check the cut-over plan — replay-based migration, not dual-write?
Replay-based from a known checkpoint. Dual-write was rejected because it would double write load.
Right.
Replay it is.
Rate limiting at the API gateway: bursts beyond the steady-state envelope get throttled or queued. This trades latency on bursts for lower steady-state cost.
And no Friday cut-overs, no pre-holiday, no PTO?
Right, all three scheduling constraints. They're a hard rule.
Got it.
Scheduling rule confirmed.

Decision: enforce rate limits at the API gateway. Steady-state limit set conservatively, bursts allowed up to the burst budget for short windows. Beyond that, requests are queued or rejected with a clear error. This lets us size the cluster smaller.
Earlier we discussed CRDT-based conflict resolution for cross-region writes. Did we ever resolve that?
It got absorbed into the 'no multi-region active-active for v1' decision. If we ever go active-active, CRDT design becomes a real conversation.
OK so CRDT is post-v1 territory.
Tied to active-active decision. Post-v1.
Rate limiting decision recorded. Steady-state and burst budgets to be defined as part of capacity planning.
What about NebulaGraph? It's open source from China, allegedly scales well.
NebulaGraph has good benchmarks but the documentation outside Chinese is patchy. Operational support for an English-speaking team would be a challenge.
Documentation matters. JanusGraph stays.
Nebula not pursued.
On observability: we already talked SLOs. What about tracing? Online lookups need distributed tracing for debugging latency outliers.
What if we built a query plan analyzer that warns developers about expensive queries before they ship?
Static query analysis on Gremlin/Cypher is hard because the cost depends on data shapes. Best-effort tools exist but they're imperfect.
Sounds like nice-to-have but not essential.
Nice-to-have, post-v1.
Distributed tracing via OpenTelemetry, sampled at 1% in production with 100% sampling on requests above the 95th percentile latency. This catches the tail without paying full tracing cost.
GDPR erasure still propagates within one snapshot window?
Yes, 5 minutes worst-case for analytical. Online is immediate.
5 minutes for analytical, immediate for online.
Confirmed.
OTel with 1% baseline and tail-sampled at p95+. Recorded. Tracing data goes where? Jaeger, our internal observability stack, or somewhere else?
I had an earlier idea about using ScyllaDB instead of Cassandra. Same wire protocol, better per-node throughput. Why didn't we pick it?
Operational maturity. Cassandra is well-understood; Scylla is a smaller ecosystem and we'd be the first in the org to run it. The throughput edge wasn't worth the unknown.
Right. We default to the more boring choice when capabilities are similar.
Cassandra it is. Scylla shelved indefinitely.
Use the org's existing observability stack rather than introducing Jaeger. That avoids adding another component to the operational footprint, which is consistent with the simpler-stack thread.
What about AllegroGraph? Mature semantic-web graph DB.
AllegroGraph is RDF/SPARQL-focused like Stardog. Different problem domain. We're property-graph, not semantic-web.
Right, wrong tool.
AllegroGraph not relevant.
Yes, send tracing to the org observability stack. No new tracing infra. Consistent with minimizing operational tax.
Out of curiosity: what would the system look like if we wrote it in Rust instead of using the JVM-based JanusGraph?
Rust would mean writing our own graph engine. The engineering cost is enormous — we'd be a graph-database company instead of a customer-facing analytics company. Not viable.
Yeah, that was just curiosity. Not a real proposal.
Right. JanusGraph stays.
Recorded. What about metrics — Prometheus or what the org uses?
How does our setup compare to Stardog, Ontotext, or other commercial graph platforms? Anything we're missing?

Stardog and Ontotext target the semantic-web / RDF / SPARQL world. Strong on inference and reasoning, weaker on the raw-edge-count throughput we need. Our property-graph workload doesn't benefit from their semantic strengths.
So they're solving a different problem. Not applicable.
Different problem domain. No action.
What the org uses. Same principle: no new metric infra. Custom metrics for JanusGraph queue depth, snapshot freshness, divergence rate go into the existing pipeline.
And we're not adding Gremlin to the analytical side?
Correct. SQL only on analytical. Gremlin not pursued.
Right.
SQL-only on analytical.
Metrics through existing org pipeline. Custom metrics for the graph-specific signals. Recorded.
And online lookups have to be under 50ms at p99?
Yes, p99 under 50ms, p50 under 10ms, sustained at 5k QPS with bursts to 20k.
Right.
That's the latency budget.
On alerting: page on freshness >10min, p99 >100ms sustained (2x SLO), error rate >1% sustained. Alert at lower thresholds without paging.
Going back to authentication: we considered using API keys instead of OAuth for service-to-service. Why did we pick mTLS in the end?

API keys are bearer tokens — anyone with the key has access. mTLS ties identity to a certificate that's harder to exfiltrate and easier to rotate. For service-to-service inside our infrastructure, mTLS is the higher bar.
Yeah, mTLS for service-to-service is correct. API keys would be a regression.
mTLS for the ingestion service stays. API keys are not on the table.
Page thresholds: freshness >10min, p99 >100ms sustained, error rate >1% sustained. Lower-severity alerts without paging at SLO breach. Recorded.
Are we still encrypting both at rest and in transit?
Yes, both. Cassandra and Parquet at rest, all client connections in transit. KMS for keys.
OK.
Encryption confirmed.
About 'sustained' — what's the window? 5 minutes? 10? We don't want a single spike to page someone at 3 AM.
Random idea: integrate with our user-data warehouse via change-data-capture so the warehouse knows when a user is erased.
CDC integration with the warehouse is a separate project. The warehouse team has their own pipelines.
Right, separate team's problem.
Out of scope.
Industry standard is 5-10 minute sustained for paging on rate-based alerts. Let's say 10 minutes — gives time for transient issues to resolve, doesn't let a real problem fester.
What about Amazon Neptune? Managed graph DB on AWS, supports both Gremlin and SPARQL.
Neptune at 50B edges would be expensive — their pricing is per-instance-hour and we'd need a large instance. Also vendor lock-in to AWS, which has come up in other contexts as a concern.
Both are real issues. Plus the proprietary Gremlin extensions wouldn't migrate. Not pursuing.
Neptune not pursued for cost and lock-in reasons.
Sustained = 10 minutes. Pages fire for sustained breaches, alerts fire on shorter windows. Recorded.
And we're at 99.9% availability target, no multi-region active-active?
Right, three nines, single-region. Multi-region was discussed and explicitly deferred.
Got it.
Three nines stands.
Alert configuration recorded. Anything on runbooks?
I'm curious about edge weights — do we support weighted edges in the schema?
JanusGraph supports properties on edges, so weights are just a property. No special weighted-edge plumbing needed; queries that care about weights filter on the property.
Good, no special case needed.
Weights are just edge properties.
Every page must link to a runbook. Page without runbook is not allowed. The runbook gives the on-call a starting point regardless of how deep the underlying issue is.
What's the on-call rotation look like for this system?
On-call structure is a people/process question. The team that builds it should own first-line.
Yeah, that's an org conversation. Park.
Parked.
Hard rule: no page without a linked runbook. Recorded as an ops constraint.
Did we ever get to a decision on whether to store derived metrics (vertex degree, page-rank, etc.) in the graph itself or compute them on demand?
It got absorbed into the 'analytical layer for any non-trivial computation' principle. We don't pre-compute derived metrics in the online store; analytical handles it.
Right, online stays narrow.
Derived metrics go through analytical.
And runbooks must be tested. We'll do quarterly game-day exercises — simulate a freshness breach, a hot-shard, a Cassandra node loss, see how the on-call handles it.
I saw a paper about using LSM-tree variants tuned for graph workloads. Anyone tried that?
It's a research direction. Production-ready implementations are scarce. RocksDB-with-tuning is the closest mainstream answer.
Research-grade stuff isn't where we should be.
Mainstream LSM via Cassandra/RocksDB is enough.
Quarterly game-day exercises covering at least: freshness breach, hot shard, Cassandra node loss. Decision recorded.
Just confirming — no Redis cache in v1, right?
Right. Default-no on Redis in v1. Reconsider only if production measurements force it.
OK.
Confirmed.
Last cluster of topics: cost-vs-availability. What's our target availability for the online store? Three nines, four nines?
What's the bus-factor on this whole thing? If two people get hit by buses, is the system maintainable?
Honestly, bus-factor is probably 2 right now. That's not a system property, it's an org property. We need documentation and cross-training as part of the rollout.
Add it to my mental list. Not blocking.
Noted.

Three nines (99.9%) is ~43 minutes downtime/month. Four nines (99.99%) is ~4 minutes/month. The former is achievable with our current architecture; the latter requires multi-region active-active with all the complexity that brings.
What about ArangoDB? Multi-model, has good graph performance.
ArangoDB is solid but the operational story at our scale is less mature than Cassandra. We'd be relying on the multi-model promise that we don't actually need — we'd use it as a graph store, period.
Don't pay for capabilities we won't use.
ArangoDB not picked.
Three nines is the right target. Four nines would mean active-active across regions, which doubles our cost and breaks the simplicity argument. If the product needs four nines later we revisit.
I'm wondering about the cost of running shadow queries continuously.
Shadow queries are a tiny fraction of production traffic — every 10 minutes against a sample. Cost is negligible.
Right, negligible cost.
Shadow queries are cheap.
Availability target: 99.9% for online store. Active-active multi-region explicitly out of scope for v1. Recorded.
We had a whole thread early on about using ElasticSearch as a secondary index. Why did we drop it?

ES would have given us flexible full-text search but JanusGraph composite indexes cover our enumerated query patterns. Adding ES would be a second data store to keep in sync, which violates the simpler-stack principle.
Right, sync between two stores is its own problem. ES stays off the table.
ES not pursued.
Analytical availability — do we promise an availability number, or is it best-effort?
Anyone want to argue for Cosmos DB with the Gremlin API?
Cosmos pricing is famously expensive at high throughput. The Gremlin API on Cosmos has some quirks vs reference Gremlin. And vendor lock-in to Azure.
Cost plus lock-in is what killed Neptune for us. Same applies to Cosmos.
Cosmos not pursued.
Analytical is best-effort, no formal availability SLO. Data team users tolerate some downtime; what they don't tolerate is silent staleness. The freshness SLO is their real availability proxy.
Just to make sure I have it straight — the analytical staleness window is 5 minutes, right?
Yes, 5 minutes is the hard requirement from the data team. We refresh the Parquet snapshot every 5 minutes from the Cassandra source of truth.
Got it.
Confirmed.
Analytical: best-effort availability, no SLO. Freshness SLO serves as the user-facing reliability signal. Recorded.
Earlier we floated using Redis with TTL-based invalidation. I want to circle back: TTL invalidation has the staleness window problem we discussed but it's operationally simple.
TTL-based invalidation is simple but you accept up to TTL seconds of staleness on cached entries. For online lookups that's hard to swallow.
Yeah, the staleness window is a problem at our latency budget. Let's drop the TTL idea. We already decided default-no on Redis anyway.
Right, that's superseded by the default-no Redis decision. Setting this aside.
OK, that's a lot of ground covered. Want me to recap the new decisions and open items from this session?
Do analytical users have a way to know if their query result is stale relative to the latest snapshot?
We expose the snapshot timestamp in the query response metadata.
OK, so transparency at the query level. Good.
Snapshot timestamp in response metadata.
Yes please. New decisions and open items from today, not the whole architecture.
Has anyone thought about how we'd handle a malformed-data ingestion incident? Bad data from upstream that corrupts a chunk of the graph.
We'd roll back to the last clean Cassandra snapshot and replay good data from the write log. Recovery time depends on how far back the corruption goes.
Not v1 priority but a real operational concern. Park it.
Parked for ops planning.

New from this session: 15-node static JanusGraph cluster, local NVMe with RF=3, 14-day daily + 12-month monthly snapshot retention, API-gateway rate limiting, OTel distributed tracing 1%+p95 tail, organizational observability stack (no new infra), page thresholds and 10-minute sustained windows, no-page-without-runbook rule, quarterly game-day exercises, 99.9% online availability target, no formal analytical SLO. Open items: minimum cache hit rate, current legacy cost for justification.
Earlier draft of the rollout plan had a phased canary — 1% traffic, then 10%, then 50%. We dropped that for the cut-over approach. Why again?
Canary doesn't help when the new system is functionally different at the data layer. The data has to be identical at cut-over time; you can't canary that. Validation gates replace canary in our case.
Right. Canary is for compute-layer changes, not data-layer cut-overs.
Cut-over with validation gates stands.
Next session — let's tackle security threat modeling. We have a graph of user data plus a federated query layer. What can go wrong?
Has anyone here used Memgraph? In-memory graph DB, supposedly very fast.
Memgraph is fast in-memory but the in-memory footprint at 50B edges is prohibitive. We'd need terabytes of RAM. Not realistic for our budget.
Yeah, 50B edges in RAM is out. Memgraph would be great at maybe 1B.
Memgraph shelved at our scale.

Major threat categories for this system: 1) authentication bypass on the online API, 2) authorization bypass via crafted queries that escape the row-level filter, 3) injection attacks in the analytical SQL path, 4) data exfiltration via the analytical export, 5) cross-tenant data leakage in the graph itself, 6) supply-chain attacks on JanusGraph or Cassandra dependencies.
