Have you ever seen anyone use Datalog as the query language for a system like this?
Datalog is elegant for graph reasoning but most teams find it too unfamiliar to maintain. There are systems like Datomic that use it. Not relevant to our pick though.
Just wondering. Moving on.
Sure.
Six is the big one for me. We pull JanusGraph from open source. What's our supply-chain story?
What about edge-compute caching? Could we put a JanusGraph read replica at edge POPs to reduce latency for geographically distributed users?

Edge replicas for graph data are a significant operational complexity multiplier. And our users aren't geographically distributed in a way that would benefit — most traffic is from one or two regions.
Then it's overkill. Not pursuing.
Edge caching shelved.

Standard supply-chain hardening: pin dependency versions, mirror artifacts internally rather than pulling from upstream at build time, scan for known CVEs continuously, sign all internal artifacts. Plus a software bill of materials for every release.
Cluster size is 15 nodes, static, no autoscaling?
Yes, 15 nodes sized for the 20k burst. No storage-layer autoscaling.
15 static nodes.
Confirmed.
Hard rule: all third-party artifacts are mirrored internally; no upstream pulls at build or runtime. SBOM generated for every release.
Are the validation gates for cut-over still all three required?
Yes: bit-exact match on holdout, 1.5x throughput, 7-day soak. All three are hard gates.
OK, three hard gates.
Three hard gates confirmed.
Constraint recorded: internal mirror for all third-party artifacts; SBOM per release.
Could we use bloom filters somewhere to short-circuit existence queries?
JanusGraph already uses bloom filters in its Cassandra storage layer. They're transparent — we benefit without doing anything specific.
Good, already covered.
Bloom filters are in there by default.
On the row-level filter: what's the actual implementation? If it's a layer above the graph, a bug in it lets users see anyone's data.
Have we costed out a chaos-engineering practice? Periodic node kills, network partitions, etc.
Chaos engineering is best-practice but adds engineering load. For v1 we'd probably stop at game-day exercises and add chaos-engineering tooling later.
Game-days first, chaos automation later. Fine.
Agreed.

Two patterns: filter in the API layer (simpler, single point of failure), or filter in the data layer via JanusGraph access control extensions (deeper but more complex). API-layer filter is the standard but the whole row-level guarantee rides on it being correct.
Just confirming the 14-day daily plus 12-month monthly retention?
Right. 14 days at daily granularity, then monthly snapshots out to 12 months.
Got it.
Retention policy stands.

API-layer filter. We need defense-in-depth: the filter is applied in code, AND every query passes through a query validator that explicitly checks the filter is present. Two layers must fail for unauthorized access.
Do we have a story for graceful degradation if Cassandra has a partial outage?

Cassandra with RF=3 tolerates one node down per replication group transparently. Beyond that, we'd be serving stale/partial data. Graceful degradation policy isn't formalized — we'd need to decide whether to fail open or fail closed.
Another formalization gap. Park it.
Parked.
Defense-in-depth: API-layer row filter PLUS query-validator gate that fails closed if the filter is missing. Recorded.
There was an earlier proposal to use Kafka as the write log so we could replay. We went with something else, right?
Cassandra's commit log effectively serves as our write log via the incremental backup mechanism. Adding Kafka would be a parallel system for the same purpose.
Yeah, don't duplicate. Cassandra's log is enough.
Kafka not added.
Cross-tenant leakage in the graph: even with the row filter, a multi-hop query starting from user A's vertex might traverse to user B's vertex through shared structures. Is that leakage?
Anyone thought about how this system would migrate if we changed cloud providers?
Cassandra and DuckDB are portable. JanusGraph is portable. The architecture is largely cloud-agnostic by design.
Good, that wasn't accidental.
Cloud-agnostic by design.

Depends. If user B's vertex contains identifiable data that should not be exposed to A, yes. If the shared structure is anonymous (like a tag node or category), no. The graph schema needs to mark which vertex types are user-private and which are shared.
At some point we floated the idea of a write-through cache that synchronously writes to both Cassandra and Redis. Why didn't that fly?
Write-through means every write pays double latency and you have a coherence problem if one side fails. Plus we now have default-no on Redis anyway.
Right, both problems. Dropped.
Write-through cache shelved.
Constraint: vertex schema must mark each vertex type as either 'user-private' or 'shared'. Queries crossing a user-private boundary without explicit permission must be blocked at the query validator.
I keep wondering about disaster-recovery RTO/RPO targets. Have we written those down?
We haven't actually written formal DR targets. The implicit assumption is the Cassandra RF=3 plus object-storage backups give us 'good enough' DR, but we haven't measured.
We should formalize that eventually. Adding to my mental list, not blocking anything.
Noted, not actioned.
Vertex-type privacy marking enforced at query validator. Recorded.
Just to verify — Spark is out, right?
Right, Spark is dropped. DuckDB handles our analytical layer.
OK.
Confirmed.
On data exfiltration via analytical export: a malicious data-team user could export everything. Do we limit export size, rate, or content?
Hugegraph? Another open-source Chinese option.
Hugegraph is interesting but smaller community than JanusGraph and less English documentation.
Same answer as Nebula. Not pursuing.
Hugegraph not pursued.
All three are reasonable: row-count cap per export, rate limit on exports per user, content classification that flags PII exports for review. Together they make bulk exfiltration noisy.
Going back to the schema-versioning discussion: I had drafted a proposal where every vertex carries a schema version. We did something simpler in the end?
Per-vertex schema version is heavyweight. We landed on a single global migration log version stored in a control vertex.
Right, additive rule means we don't need per-vertex versions.
Global migration version stands.
Decision: analytical exports capped at 1M rows per query, rate limited to 5 exports per hour per user, all exports logged with user id and timestamp, PII exports trigger a flag for security review.
I remember we briefly considered ZeroMQ for the ingestion-to-storage path. Why isn't that in the final picture?
ZeroMQ is excellent at low-latency message passing but doesn't give us durability. We need durable queuing so we landed on a managed durable queue with at-least-once semantics.
Right, we need durability. ZeroMQ stays out.
ZMQ rejected on durability grounds.
Export controls: 1M row cap per query, 5 exports per hour per user, mandatory logging, PII flag for review. Recorded.
OrientDB? It's been around forever, multi-model like Arango.
OrientDB's been in maintenance-mode for years. The community has thinned. Not a safe bet for new infrastructure.
Right, hard pass.
OrientDB rejected on ecosystem health.
Injection on the analytical SQL path: data team users write arbitrary SQL. What stops them from issuing destructive statements?
Have we thought about what happens if Cassandra has a poison-pill query that takes down a node?
Cassandra has some query-killer capabilities but they're imperfect. We'd want to add query monitoring at the JanusGraph layer to catch pathological queries before they hit Cassandra.
Probably an ops concern more than design-time. Park it.
Parked.

DuckDB itself is read-only on the Parquet files. There's no write path the SQL session can exploit. But SQL injection-like issues can still happen if our query frontend builds SQL strings from untrusted input. Solution: parameterized queries everywhere, never string concatenation.
If a partner contract changes mid-quarter, how fast can we update their scope?
View definitions are quick to update. New scope is live within hours.
Hours for live scope changes. Good.
Fast partner-scope changes possible.
Hard rule: no SQL string concatenation in the analytical query frontend. All queries via parameterized form.
What about RedisGraph? Redis with a graph module on top, all in-memory.
RedisGraph is fast in-memory but Redis itself was abandoned by Redis Labs for graph use cases — they shut it down in 2023. So it's not a live option.
Oh, didn't know it was discontinued. Hard pass then.
RedisGraph dead. Not pursued.
Parameterized queries only on the analytical frontend. Recorded.
Has anyone here used Dgraph in production?
Dgraph has interesting GraphQL-native design. Operational reports are mixed at scale. The company had instability issues in 2022-2023 which affects long-term confidence.
Vendor stability is a real concern. Not safe to bet on for new infra.
Dgraph not pursued on stability grounds.
What about authentication bypass on the online REST API? If our auth layer has a bug, the whole system is open.
Early in the discussion we talked about exposing a streaming API for real-time updates to analytical clients. We dropped that, right?
Real-time analytical updates is on the explicitly-deferred list (D26). Clients accept the 5-minute snapshot window.
Right, that's post-v1.
Streaming API deferred.

Standard mitigations: defense-in-depth (verify auth at the gateway AND the service), short-lived tokens, rate-limit failed auth attempts, audit-log every auth decision. Plus periodic auth penetration testing.
Have we considered offering a GraphQL gateway in front of the REST API for clients who want declarative queries?
GraphQL on top of our constrained REST would be a thin shim. It would add a layer to operate without adding new capabilities. Most clients are internal services that already use REST.
Not worth it then.
GraphQL not pursued.

Auth: verified at both gateway and service, short-lived tokens (15-minute TTL), failed-auth rate limit triggers IP block after 10 failures in 5 minutes, every auth decision logged, quarterly penetration testing of the auth path.
What if we open-sourced the parts of our system that aren't customer-specific?
Open-sourcing is a legal and product decision more than a technical one. Not in scope of this design conversation.
Right, that's an exec call. Not here.
Out of scope.
Auth constraints: gateway+service double-check, 15-min TTL tokens, 10-in-5-min IP block, full auth audit log, quarterly pentest. Recorded.
If we have to do a full data re-ingest from upstream, what's the timeline?
At our bulk-load throughput, re-ingesting 50B edges would take days.
Days. Good to know.
Days bound recorded informally.
We should also think about insider threats. What stops an engineer with prod access from running a wide query and exporting it?
What about temporal queries — 'show me the graph as it was a week ago'?

Temporal queries on a graph that doesn't track per-vertex history would require expensive reconstruction from snapshots. We're not building that in v1; daily snapshots give partial time-travel for analytical.
Daily snapshots are enough for now.
Temporal queries via snapshot history. No first-class temporal.

Hard problem. Best you can do: role-based access (engineers don't get prod data access by default), break-glass procedures with logging when they do, anomaly detection on query patterns, and culture. Pure technical solutions don't fully solve it.
Are we still going with JanusGraph or did we revisit that?
Still JanusGraph. The decision stood through the various sanity checks — Postgres-with-AGE wouldn't scale, Neo4j cost was too high. Production rollout is conditional on operational expertise.
OK, still JanusGraph then.
Yes.

Engineers don't have prod data access by default. Break-glass for incident response, logged and reviewed. Anomaly detection on query patterns flags unusual access. Cultural reinforcement that prod data is sensitive.
Going back to the snapshot frequency question — at one point I was thinking 1-minute snapshots for tighter freshness. Why did we land on 5 minutes again?
1-minute snapshots produce ~5x the snapshot volume. Storage cost and snapshot pipeline load become problematic. 5 minutes was the compromise the data team accepted.
Right. 1-minute would have been nicer but the data team OK'd 5. We're not revisiting that.
Five minutes stands.
Insider-threat mitigations: default no engineer prod data access, audited break-glass, query anomaly detection, cultural emphasis. Recorded.
Random thought: what if we used a graph neural network to predict hot paths and pre-warm the cache?

ML-based cache warming exists in research papers but in practice the deterministic 'most-recently-used' or 'most-frequently-used' heuristics get you 90% of the way. ML adds operational complexity for marginal gain.
Yeah, not for v1. Maybe v3 if we have the data to train it.
Deferred indefinitely.
Threat modeling done for now. Let's switch to partner integrations. We have one partner — Alpha — that wants direct read access to a slice of the graph.
Do we have a plan for what happens when JanusGraph releases a major version update?
Major version updates of a production data system are always painful. We'd do a parallel cluster with replay-based migration, similar to our initial cut-over plan.
OK, but we don't need to plan that for v1. It's a future-team problem.
Future problem.
Partner integration is its own design problem. Do they get a separate cluster, a subset of the existing analytical layer, or a federated query interface?
Random thought: what if we used homomorphic encryption so the cloud provider can't see our graph data even if they wanted to?
Homomorphic encryption for graph operations is hundreds of times slower than plaintext. Latency budget would be destroyed.
Yeah, the latency tradeoff makes it a non-starter.
HE shelved.
I don't want to spin up a separate cluster — that's another full stack to operate. A subset of the analytical layer with strict scoping seems right.
Do we have any sense of what happens if our partner Alpha onboards 10x the queries we expected?
Their rate limit at the gateway would kick in. We'd see lots of 429s on their end and not be impacted ourselves.
Right, technical answer is fine. Contract is a separate conversation.
Tech side handled by rate limits.
Subset approach: a partner-specific DuckDB view that filters to only the data Alpha is contractually entitled to see. Partner authenticates via their own credentials, hits a partner-scoped endpoint.
Just want to double-check the cut-over plan — replay-based migration, not dual-write?
Replay-based from a known checkpoint. Dual-write was rejected because it would double write load.
Right.
Replay it is.
Decision: partner data access via a partner-scoped DuckDB view layer. No direct Cassandra access. Partner credentials are issued through our regular auth flow with a 'partner' role.
And no Friday cut-overs, no pre-holiday, no PTO?
Right, all three scheduling constraints. They're a hard rule.
Got it.
Scheduling rule confirmed.
Partner access architecture: partner-scoped DuckDB views, normal auth with partner role, no Cassandra access. Recorded.
Earlier we discussed CRDT-based conflict resolution for cross-region writes. Did we ever resolve that?
It got absorbed into the 'no multi-region active-active for v1' decision. If we ever go active-active, CRDT design becomes a real conversation.
OK so CRDT is post-v1 territory.
Tied to active-active decision. Post-v1.
What about rate limits for partners? They might hammer the analytical layer harder than our internal users.
What about NebulaGraph? It's open source from China, allegedly scales well.
NebulaGraph has good benchmarks but the documentation outside Chinese is patchy. Operational support for an English-speaking team would be a challenge.
Documentation matters. JanusGraph stays.
Nebula not pursued.
Partner-specific rate limits, contractually negotiated, enforced at the gateway. Default to a conservative limit and negotiate higher for paying tiers.
What if we built a query plan analyzer that warns developers about expensive queries before they ship?
Static query analysis on Gremlin/Cypher is hard because the cost depends on data shapes. Best-effort tools exist but they're imperfect.
Sounds like nice-to-have but not essential.
Nice-to-have, post-v1.
Partner rate limits: contractually defined per partner, enforced at the gateway, default conservative.
GDPR erasure still propagates within one snapshot window?
Yes, 5 minutes worst-case for analytical. Online is immediate.
5 minutes for analytical, immediate for online.
Confirmed.
Partner rate limits via contract + gateway enforcement. Recorded.
I had an earlier idea about using ScyllaDB instead of Cassandra. Same wire protocol, better per-node throughput. Why didn't we pick it?
Operational maturity. Cassandra is well-understood; Scylla is a smaller ecosystem and we'd be the first in the org to run it. The throughput edge wasn't worth the unknown.
Right. We default to the more boring choice when capabilities are similar.
Cassandra it is. Scylla shelved indefinitely.
Audit requirements for partner queries — partners may need to prove what they accessed for their own compliance.
What about AllegroGraph? Mature semantic-web graph DB.
AllegroGraph is RDF/SPARQL-focused like Stardog. Different problem domain. We're property-graph, not semantic-web.
Right, wrong tool.
AllegroGraph not relevant.
We can offer them a queryable audit log of their own queries. Don't expose other partners' or internal users' queries. Each partner sees only their own access history.
Out of curiosity: what would the system look like if we wrote it in Rust instead of using the JVM-based JanusGraph?
Rust would mean writing our own graph engine. The engineering cost is enormous — we'd be a graph-database company instead of a customer-facing analytics company. Not viable.
Yeah, that was just curiosity. Not a real proposal.
Right. JanusGraph stays.
Partner audit: each partner can query their own access history through an audit endpoint. No visibility into other partners or internal access.
How does our setup compare to Stardog, Ontotext, or other commercial graph platforms? Anything we're missing?

Stardog and Ontotext target the semantic-web / RDF / SPARQL world. Strong on inference and reasoning, weaker on the raw-edge-count throughput we need. Our property-graph workload doesn't benefit from their semantic strengths.
So they're solving a different problem. Not applicable.
Different problem domain. No action.
Partner audit endpoint, scoped to their own activity. Recorded.
And we're not adding Gremlin to the analytical side?
Correct. SQL only on analytical. Gremlin not pursued.
Right.
SQL-only on analytical.
If a partner is found in breach of contract — say, exfiltrating beyond their allowed scope — how do we cut them off?
And online lookups have to be under 50ms at p99?
Yes, p99 under 50ms, p50 under 10ms, sustained at 5k QPS with bursts to 20k.
Right.
That's the latency budget.
Partner credentials revocable at the gateway with immediate effect. Their views also yank-able by removing the view definition. Both should be one-command operations for fast response.
